%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Bilinear Transformation with Prewarping

> **Section Objective:** Learn how bilinear-transform frequency warping shifts cutoff frequencies, and how prewarping forces one chosen frequency to map exactly.

## Concepts In This Section

- Bilinear frequency mapping
- Frequency warping
- Cutoff-frequency miss
- Prewarping

## 1. Bilinear Frequency Mapping

The bilinear transform maps the entire continuous-time frequency axis — which runs from \(-\infty\) to \(+\infty\) in rad/s — onto the finite digital frequency range \([0, \pi]\) rad/sample on the unit circle.

This mapping is **not** a simple straight scaling. It uses a tangent/arctangent relationship, which means the output \(\Omega\) grows nonlinearly with the input \(\omega\). High analog frequencies get compressed toward \(\pi\), while low frequencies are mapped more faithfully.

### CONCRETE READING EXAMPLE

If you plug an analog cutoff \(\omega_c\) into the mapping, the output is the digital cutoff \(\Omega_c\). But because the mapping is nonlinear, \(\Omega_c\) will generally **not** equal \(\omega_c T\) — the simple-scaling approximation fails except at very low frequencies.

#### Note
Use \(\omega\) for continuous-time frequency in rad/s and \(\Omega\) for digital frequency in rad/sample throughout this section.

## 2. Prewarping fixes one important frequency

**Analog-to-digital frequency mapping under the bilinear transform.**

- \(\Omega\) — digital frequency, in rad/sample
- \(\omega\) — continuous-time frequency, in rad/s
- \(T\) — the mapping parameter used in the bilinear transform design

**When to use it:** Whenever an analog frequency must be mapped into the digital system — for example, to find where an analog cutoff lands after bilinear transformation.

**Exam trigger:** A problem gives \(\omega_c\), sampling information, and asks where the cutoff lands after bilinear transformation. Plug in and evaluate.

**Common misuse:** Replacing this nonlinear arctangent mapping with the simple approximation \(\Omega = \omega T\). That approximation is only valid when \(\omega T \ll 1\); at typical audio or signal-processing cutoff frequencies it introduces significant error.

$$\Omega = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2}\right)$$

$$\omega = \frac{2}{T}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega}{2}\right)$$
***Inverse frequency mapping: digital to analog.**

- \(\omega\) — continuous-time frequency, in rad/s
- \(\Omega\) — digital frequency, in rad/sample
- \(T\) — the bilinear-transform mapping parameter

**When to use it:** When designing an analog prototype from a required digital cutoff. You specify the desired \(\Omega_c\) and use this formula to find the analog prototype cutoff \(\omega_c\) that the prototype filter must meet.

**Exam trigger:** A desired digital cutoff is specified and the analog prototype cutoff must be chosen before applying the bilinear transform.

**Common misuse:** Treating \(\omega\) and \(\Omega\) as if they had the same units. They do not — \(\omega\) is in rad/s and \(\Omega\) is in rad/sample. Mixing them produces dimensionally incorrect results.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

$$\Omega_c = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega_c T}{2}\right) = 1.5116$$
***Numerical cutoff-miss example from the textbook.**

Given: \(\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot 3000\) rad/s, sampling rate \(= 10\text{ kHz}\), so \(T = 1/10000\).

Plugging into the bilinear frequency mapping:

$$\Omega_c = 2\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2\pi \cdot 3000}{2 \cdot 10000}\right) = 1.5116 \text{ rad/sample}$$

The desired cutoff from simple scaling would be \(\Omega_c = 0.6\pi \approx 1.8850\) rad/sample.

**Conclusion:** The ordinary bilinear mapping gives \(1.5116\), which is **short of** the desired \(1.8850\). This shortfall is frequency warping.

**Exam trigger:** If the computed bilinear cutoff is lower than the desired cutoff, that discrepancy is the warping effect — not an error in the formula.

**Common misuse:** Assuming the target digital cutoff is automatically preserved by the bilinear transform. It is not — the nonlinear mapping always shifts cutoffs unless prewarping is applied.*

## 2. Prewarping Fixes One Important Frequency

Because cutoff frequencies are high-stakes design points, the analog prototype can be adjusted **before** applying the bilinear transform. This adjustment is called **prewarping**.

Prewarping works by choosing the bilinear-transform parameter \(T_{\text{prewarp}}\) so that one selected continuous-time frequency \(\omega_c\) maps **exactly** to one selected digital frequency \(\Omega_c\).

### CONCRETE EXAMPLE

For the same \(3000\text{ Hz}\) cutoff and desired digital cutoff \(0.6\pi\), prewarping changes the bilinear-transform parameter to approximately \(1/6848\) — **not** \(1/10000\) (the physical sampling period). Plugging this adjusted parameter into the bilinear transform forces the \(3000\text{ Hz}\) cutoff to land exactly at \(0.6\pi\).

### TOP-SCORE WARNING

Prewarping fixes **one selected frequency only**. The rest of the frequency axis remains nonlinearly mapped. Do not assume prewarping linearizes the entire mapping.

$$T_{\text{prewarp}} = \frac{2}{\omega_c}\tan\!\left(\frac{\Omega_c}{2}\right)$$
***Prewarping formula: adjusted bilinear-transform parameter.**

- \(T_{\text{prewarp}}\) — the adjusted parameter used **inside** the bilinear transform design; it is not the physical sampling period of the target digital system
- \(\omega_c\) — the selected analog cutoff frequency in rad/s
- \(\Omega_c\) — the desired digital cutoff frequency in rad/sample

**When to use it:** When an exam or design problem states that the cutoff frequency must be accurate after bilinear transformation. Compute \(T_{\text{prewarp}}\) and substitute it in place of \(T\) in the bilinear transform.

**Numerical check:** For \(\omega_c = 2\pi \cdot 3000\) and \(\Omega_c = 0.6\pi\):

$$T_{\text{prewarp}} = \frac{2}{2\pi \cdot 3000}\tan\!\left(\frac{0.6\pi}{2}\right) \approx \frac{1}{6848}$$

**Common misuse 1:** Thinking prewarping changes the physical sampling rate of the target system. It does not — it only adjusts the design parameter for the chosen frequency.

**Common misuse 2:** Thinking one prewarp value makes the entire frequency axis linear. It corrects exactly one point; the rest of the axis remains nonlinear.*

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Bilinear mapping \(\Omega=2\tan^{-1}(\omega T/2)\) compresses the infinite analog axis onto \([0,\pi]\) nonlinearly.
- Inverse mapping \(\omega=(2/T)\tan(\Omega/2)\) finds the analog prototype cutoff for a required digital cutoff.
- Example: ordinary mapping gives \(\Omega_c=1.5116\), short of the desired \(0.6\pi\approx1.8850\) — this gap is frequency warping.
- Prewarping formula \(T_{\text{prewarp}}=(2/\omega_c)\tan(\Omega_c/2)\) forces one selected cutoff to land exactly; the rest of the axis stays nonlinear.

*Next, this prewarping idea is applied inside a complete Butterworth filter transformation.*
